segments.s527-g65: برین is the compound بر + این (here written together), meaning "on/upon this"; هم adds the meaning "also/likewise." نشان is a noun "sign/standard/mark." تا here means "as far as," and به شهر صطخر = "to the city Istakhr." Word order is literary/poetic (preposed modifier برین هم نشان before the destination phrase).
segments.s527-g66: که introduces a relative clause "where/which." بهمن is the personal name Bahman. بدو is the enclitic prepositional pronoun meaning "to it/to him" (referring back to the city). داشت is simple past "had." آواز و فخر are two nouns in apposition meaning "fame (reputation) and pride/glory."
segments.s527-g67: ز گیتی چو is a poetic adverbial phrase: ز (= from/through) گیتی (= the world) چو (= when). برخاست is the past tense "rose/arose." آواز شاه is the noun phrase "the king's voice" (noun before possessor, a common poetic order).
segments.s527-g68: ز هر سو = "from every side" (ز = from, هر سو = every side). بپیوست is the past/preterite form meaning "joined/attached." بی‌مر is an old/uncertain adjective placed before سپاه (army); here it qualifies سپاه, and سپاه is the head noun "army/host." The clause thus says the army united/joined from all sides. (بی‌مر is archaic/obscure in meaning.)
segments.s527-g69: مر is an archaic/clitic form meaning "to me" (classical object form); او را is the object phrase "him" with the definite object marker را; فراوان is an adjective/adverb "abundant(ly);" نمودند is the past-tense verb "they showed/made apparent." گنج is the direct object "treasure." The sequence is literary and allows multiple objects/recipients in classical ordering.
segments.s527-g70: کجا is a relative/adverbial "where." بهمن is the personal name Bahman. آگنده is the adjective "filled/filled up." بود is the past of "to be" (here past-state "was"). آن is the demonstrative "that/it." به before رنج has an instrumental/causal sense "by/with," and رنج means "toil/hardship." Grammatically the clause explains that Bahman had filled that place (or treasury) by means of toil.
segments.s527-g71: درمهای آگنده = "the abundant/filled dirhams" (درمهای is the plural genitive/construct of dirham; آگنده qualifies it). را is the definite object marker. برفشاند is a past/transitive verb meaning "he scattered/strewn (them)." The subject is omitted in the line (typical in poetry) and must be inferred from context.
segments.s527-g72: به نیرو = "by/with force/strength" (به used adverbially), شد is the past form "became/it happened" or auxiliary introducing the resulting action, از پارس لشکر = "the army from Pars" (از = from/of, پارس = Pārs/Persia, لشکر = army), and براند is the present/subjunctive/old form of راندن meaning "to drive away/put to flight." Together the clause means that by force the army of Pars was made to drive (them) away or that the Parsian host drove them off.
segments.s527-g9: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. چشمش is the possessed noun 'his gaze/eye' with the enclitic ـش; به روی is a prepositional phrase 'to/towards the face (of)' and سپهبد is the object of that preposition. رسید is a past tense verb 'reached/arrived' placed at the end: literally 'when his eye reached the face of the spahbed' = 'when his gaze fell upon the spahbed.'
segments.s527-g10: ز باره درآمد is a poetic phrasing: ز + باره ('from/at the occasion') + درآمد ('came forth/appeared'), i.e. 'from that occasion there came forth' or 'a reaction arose.' چنانچون is a comparative 'just as/just so,' and سزید is the past/impersonal sense of سزایدن ('to befit/to deserve'), here meaning 'as was fitting' or 'as he deserved.'
segments.s527-g11: بیامد is a past verb 'he came.' دمان پای او is a compound noun phrase 'the hem (دمان) at his foot' (i.e. the skirt at his feet) with the possessive او. بوس داد is a light-verb construction: بوس (kiss) + داد ('gave') = 'gave a kiss' → 'kissed (it).' Word order places verbs at the end, and the object phrase (دمان پای او) precedes the verb.
segments.s527-g12: ز ساسانیان is a prepositional phrase 'of/from the Sasanians' (poetic ز for از) indicating the topic or source of remembrance. بیشتر کرد یاد is a causative/colloquial ordering meaning 'made more remembrance' → 'remembered more' or 'recalled more strongly.' کرد is the light verb 'made/did' combining with یاد (remembrance) to form 'remembered.'
segments.s527-g13: فراوان is an adverb 'frequently/plentifully' modifying the verb. جهانجوی is an agent noun ('the world-seeker' or 'the ambitious one') serving as the subject. بنواختش is past tense with the prefix بـ + نواخت (to strike/play) plus the enclitic ـش 'him' → 'struck him (repeatedly).' Persian permits the adverb before subject/verb in poetic order.
segments.s527-g14: به زود آمدن is a noun phrase formed with the infinitive آمدن 'coming' and modifiers 'به زود' ('with/for quickness' = 'promptly'). ارج is 'honor/worth,' and بشناختش is the past tense verb بشناخت + enclitic ـش 'he recognized him.' The whole phrase means 'he recognized his merit/valor for having come promptly.'
segments.s527-g15: پراندیشه شد is a compound predicate 'became pensive/worried' (پراندیشه = full of thought/worry; شد = became). نامجوی is the subject (here a personal name). از + سباک indicates cause or source ('because of Sabak' or 'on account of Sabak'). The clause thus means 'Namjuy became anxious because of Sabak.' Poetic word order places the predicate before the subject.
segments.s527-g16: دلش گشت is 'his heart turned / became' (دل 'heart' + ـش 'his' + گشت 'became'). زان پیر 'from that old man' (زان = 'from that/therefore' referring back; پیر = 'old man'). پر بیم و باک is an adjectival phrase 'full of fear and dread' (پر = full (of) + بیم 'fear' و باک 'and dread'). The clause means 'his heart became full of fear and dread of that old man.'
segments.s527-g49: سپاه is the (collective) subject 'the army/host'; از دو رویه is an adverbial phrase 'from two sides' (literal 'from two surfaces/faces') placed after the subject. کشیدند is a past plural transitive verb 'drew up' (کشیدن used here in the sense 'to draw [a line/formation]'); صف is the direct object 'the line/formation'. The plural verb agrees with the collective noun in poetry.
segments.s527-g50: This is an elliptic nominal clause describing the troops' state: همه 'all/everyone' quantifies the fighters; نیزه و تیغ هندی is a coordinate noun phrase 'spear and (Indian) sword' where هندی postmodifies تیغ; به کف is a locative phrase 'in (their) hand'. No finite verb is present; the whole phrase functions as a descriptive supplement to the previous clause.
segments.s527-g51: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when/as'; شیران is plural 'lions' used metaphorically for brave fighters; جنگی is a postpositive adjective 'warlike' modifying شیران; برآویختند is a past plural verb 'they sprang upon/they leapt' (برآویختن = to spring at or attack). Word order is typical: temporal marker + subject + adjective + verb.
segments.s527-g52: چو here means 'like/as' introducing a simile; جوی روان is a noun phrase 'a flowing stream' (جوی 'stream' modified by روان 'flowing'); خون is the object 'blood'; همی is an archaic continuous/iterative particle that softens or sustains the action (often 'kept/continually'); ریختند is the past plural of ریختن 'they poured/shed'. The clause as a whole says they shed blood as if a stream were flowing.
segments.s527-g53: بدین گونه is a fixed phrase 'in this way/thus' (here tokenized into parts); تا is the subordinating conjunction 'until'; گشت is a past/copular verb 'became/turned'; خورشید زرد is a predicative nominal phrase 'the sun yellow' (i.e. 'the sun turned yellow'). The order places the verb before its nominal predicate, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s527-g54: Two parallel verbless nominal phrases joined by و: هوا پر ز گرد 'the air full of dust' and زمین پر ز مرد 'the earth full of men'. پر is an adjective 'full (of)'; ز is the poetic genitive/preposition 'of/from'; گرد 'dust' and مرد 'men' are the nouns completing the sense. The construction is a compact, poetic way to say 'the air was filled with dust and the ground with men.'
segments.s527-g55: چو 'when' introduces the temporal clause; شد is the past tense 'became/was' (acted as copula here); چادر چرخ is a poetic genitive expression 'the tent of the sky/heaven's tent' (i.e. the dome of the sky); پیروزه‌رنگ is an adjectival compound 'turquoise-colored' postmodifying the phrase. The whole clause = 'when the sky's canopy became turquoise.'
segments.s527-g56: سپاه سباک is a noun phrase 'the nimble army' where سباک is used as an adjective after the noun; اندر آمد is a verbal compound meaning 'came in/entered' (اندر = 'into/within' + آمد 'came'); به جنگ is a prepositional phrase 'to/into battle' marking goal. The clause means 'the nimble army entered into battle.'
segments.s527-g33: 'مر' is the older/dialectal form for 'me/for me' and functions as an indirect object here; it comes first for emphasis. 'او را' is the direct object with the definite/object marker 'را'. 'به جای پدر' is a prepositional phrase 'in the place of (my) father'. 'داشتی' is 2nd-person singular past (you had/kept), so the whole clause reads 'you kept him for me in place of father.'
segments.s527-g34: 'بران' is an adverbial 'upon that / moreover / on top of that'. 'نامدارانش' is 'his notables' (نامداران + enclitic 'ش' = 'his'). 'سر' here means 'chief/leader' (not 'head' anatomically). 'داشتی' again is 2nd-person past 'you had/held', so the clause reads 'moreover you held the chief position among his notables' (i.e., you were their chief).
segments.s527-g35: 'دل شاه' is the possessed phrase 'the king's heart' (noun + possessor). 'ز' is the poetic/prepositional 'from / because of'. 'اندیشه' is 'thought / worry', and 'آزاد شد' is a compound predication: 'آزاد' (free) + 'شد' (became) — past intransitive 'became free' (i.e., was relieved).
segments.s527-g36: The adverbial/prepositional 'سوی آذر' places direction/target ('toward Azar' — here Azar is a proper name). The predicate is a compound adjectival phrase 'رام خراد' ('tame/gentle' + 'wise') followed by 'شد' ('became'), so literally 'he became gentle and wise toward Azar.' Note 'خراد' is an archaic/poetic form of 'خرد' (wisdom).
segments.s527-g37: 'نیایش بسی کرد' is 'he offered much prayer' — noun 'نیایش' (prayer) + adverb 'بسی' (much, a lot) + past verb 'کرد' (did/offered). 'پیش خدای' is the locative/prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of God' ('پیش' = in the presence of).
segments.s527-g38: 'که' introduces a purpose/result clause 'that/so that'. 'باشدش' is subjunctive/present-form 'may it be / let it be' with the enclitic '-ش' = 'for him' (i.e., 'that there be for him'). 'بر نیکوی رهنمای' links the predicate: 'بر' here marks the role/possession roughly 'a(n) ... as/to' and 'نیکوی رهنمای' = 'a good/excellent guide.' So the clause means 'that there may be for him a good guide.'
segments.s527-g39: 'به هر کار' is a simple prepositional phrase 'in/for every affair'. 'پیروزگر' here functions as 'triumphant/victorious' (an attributive noun/adjective — the thing given or the state conferred). 'داردش' is a verb with the enclitic object '-ش' meaning 'he has/keeps/ grants it to him' — in context idiomatically 'he grants him (victory)'. Word order is prepositional phrase → predicate noun → verb with enclitic object.
segments.s527-g40: 'درخت بزرگی' is a noun phrase 'a great/large tree' (بزرگی = greatness/size). The following 'به بر داردش' repeats the light-verb construction with enclitic '-ش' ('داردش') and prepositional elements 'به بر' — functionally here 'به بر داردش' means 'he bestows/sets upon him' (literally 'has it upon him'); poetically it expresses that a great tree is given/established for him. This clause therefore says he bestowed a great tree on him (metaphorical or literal depending on context).
segments.s527-g17: به راه اندرون is a prepositional phrase: به + راه + اندرون = ‘on/along the inner way/inside the palace’. نیز (‘also’) modifies the whole clause. آژیر بود is the predicate: آژیر (an alarm/shrill signal) + بود (past 3rd‑person singular 'was'), giving ‘there was an alarm’. Word order (prepositional phrase → adverb → subject → copula) is typical of poetic inversion.
segments.s527-g18: که introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause ('for/that'), followed by با او (‘with him’). سپاه جهانگیر is a noun phrase where جهانگیر functions adjectivally/postpositively to characterize سپاه (‘a world‑conquering army’). بود is again the past copula (‘was’). The clause explains the reason for the alarm.
segments.s527-g19: A sequence of epithets modifying پیر: جهاندیده (worldly‑wise) + بیدار (here 'alert') + دل ('heart', used with بیدار to mean 'alert‑hearted'), then بود (past copula) and the noun پیر ('old man/elder'). Adjectives precede the noun; بود puts the description in the past.
segments.s527-g20: بدانست is a past verb meaning ‘knew/understood (it)’; it takes the following noun phrase as its object. اندیشه here means 'intention' (i.e., intention/plan), and اردشیر is the possessor/name: 'he knew Ardashir’s intention'. The subject (the elder) is carried over from the previous clause.
segments.s527-g21: A string of terse past actions in narrative sequence: بیامد (he came — past), بیاورد (here used as a past narrative form 'he brought/caused to bring'), استا (an archaic/poetic past 'stopped/paused'), and the coordinating و ('and') before زند. These verbs are terse, narrative pasts presented in sequence; the subject is implied (the elder).
segments.s527-g22: چنین گفت is a formulaic phrase 'thus he said/so he spoke' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said' used as a narrative tag). کز here is the subordinating 'that/which', introducing what follows; کردگار بلند is a noun phrase 'the Exalted Creator' (کردگار = Creator/God, بلند = lofty/exalted). The clause likely begins a quotation or explanation.
segments.s527-g23: A compact descriptive phrase: نژندست ('vile/lowly' or 'ignoble') qualifies the person, followed by پرمایه جان ('having abundant/substantial life/body' — i.e., robust or well‑endowed in body) and سباک ('nimble/lively') describing جان (soul/spirit). The adjectives and noun combine to give a paradoxical portrait: ignoble in birth or rank but robust and lively in body/spirit.
segments.s527-g24: اگر introduces a conditional 'if/unless'. دل ('heart') is the logical subject/thing that might be lacking, and ندارد is present‑tense negative ('does not have' / 'has not' / 'is not turned'). سوی شاه پاک is a directional/relational phrase: سوی = 'toward/unto', شاه پاک = 'the pure/exalted king'. Together: 'if his heart is not toward the pure king.'
segments.s527-g25: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' (classical/poetic). آگاهی is the noun 'news/information' and آمد is the past tense 'came' (3rd person). ز here is the poetic/prepositional 'from' and the following شاه اردشیر names the source: 'from King Ardashir.' Word order is typical older Persian: conjunction + noun + verb + prepositional source.
segments.s527-g26: که introduces the subordinate content clause 'that ...'. آورد is the past tense 'brought' (3rd person). لشکر is the direct object 'army/host'. The prefixed ب (here a separate surface) marks direction 'to/into' and is joined to the following element; آبگیر is the destination 'pond/reservoir.' Together بدین = 'to this' (classical/poetic form).
segments.s527-g27: چنان is an adverb 'so/thus' intensifying the clause. سیر here carries the sense of being 'sated/tired' or 'fed up' (an adjective/noun used adjectivally). سر گشتم is a verbal phrase from گشتن 'to become/turn' (گشتم = I became), where سر functions as part of the idiomatic expression meaning to change state; از introduces the target of that feeling 'with/regarding Ardavan.' Overall: 'I became so (sore/angry/fed up) with Ardavan.'
segments.s527-g28: که introduces the consequence/result clause 'that ...'. از is the preposition 'from' or 'out of' and پیرزن is 'old woman.' گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned' and is used here intransitively: 'became (a) man.' مرد جوان is the resulting description 'young man' (مرد = man; جوان as adjective/noun 'young/young man').
segments.s527-g29: مرا is the object 'me' (written here split as م + را in the provided tokens: را is the definite/object marker placed after the pronoun). The following adjectival epithets—نیک‌پی 'well-born/virtuous' and مهربان 'kind'—describe the speaker. بنده‌دان is an epithet/noun placed after them (in older/poetic Persian nouns can follow in a chain of epithets); here it functions as an attributive label (literal lexical senses supplied are 'manacle/fetters', so the word is being used poetically as a label).
segments.s527-g30: This segment continues the string of epithets. شکیبادل is a poetic epithet 'fragrant breeze' placed in apposition; و is the conjunction 'and.' راز داننده is a noun phrase 'knower of secrets' (راز = 'secret', داننده = 'knower'). The final دان functions as an imperative/command verb 'consider/hold (me)' in this construction (مرا ... دان = 'consider me ...'), so the clause as a whole is an instruction to regard the speaker with these qualities.
segments.s527-g31: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' بشنید is the simple past 'heard' (3rd person). زو here is the contracted poetic form of از او 'from him' (provided as separate pieces in the tokens) indicating the source; اردشیر is the person named, and این سخن = 'this speech/these words' is the object of hearing. Word order places the verb early, typical in Ferdowsi's style.
segments.s527-g32: یکی دیگر is a phrase 'one other / another' (یکی = 'one', دیگر = 'other/another'). اندیشه is 'thought/plan/idea' and افگند is the past of افکندن 'threw/cast' often used metaphorically for 'formed/entertained' a thought. بن is a short poetic particle/word meaning 'root/base' or appears here adverbially/poetically; in context it closes the line and emphasizes the action of casting/setting down the new thought ('he cast another thought').
segments.s527-g57: برآمد is a past/inceptive verb meaning 'arose/issued forth' placed before its nominal description (poetic inversion). یکی باد و گردی is a compact subject phrase 'a wind and a dust-cloud' where یکی functions as an indefinite 'a/certain'. چو قیر is a simile 'like tar' modifying گردی.
segments.s527-g58: بیامد is a verb 'came' following the first clause to continue the action. ز قلب سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the heart/centre of the host' (ز + noun), with اردشیر in apposition/possessive relation identifying which army.
segments.s527-g59: بیفگند is an action verb 'he hurled/shot' (archaic poetic past). زیشان = ز + ایشان functions here as 'among them / from among them' (directing the action toward that group). فراوان modifies the verb/adverbially 'many/plentifully'. به گرز marks the weapon used or target direction 'with/at maces' (به + گرز).
segments.s527-g60: که introduces a descriptive clause that qualifies the maces/men just mentioned. با زور و دل بود is a nominal description 'they were with strength and heart' (با + noun; بود is the past copula). و با فر و برز parallels the first pair: 'and with glory and exaltation'—two paired qualities are listed in balanced poetic phrasing.
segments.s527-g61: گریزان is an active/participial adjective 'fleeing'. بشد here functions as an inchoative/past marker 'became/was' producing the sense 'he fled' when combined with گریزان; the proper name بهمن اردوان is the subject (order is inverted for poetic effect).
segments.s527-g62: تنش is 'his body' (تن + possessive -ش). خسته is a predicate adjective 'weary' modifying تنش; تیر و تیره‌روان are coordinating modifiers giving causes/conditions: 'weary by arrows and (being) ill‑fated/gloomy'—the copula is omitted, typical of epic compression.
segments.s527-g63: پس‌اندر is an adverbial phrase 'afterwards/then'. همی is an emphatic/progressive particle (common in epic Persian). تاخت is a past/action verb 'rode/charged', and شاه اردشیر is the subject following the verb (poetic inversion: verb before subject).
segments.s527-g64: This is an instrumental/ablative phrase describing how he charged: ناله بوق 'the wailing/cry of the trumpet' and باران تیر 'a shower/rain of arrows'. The initial token ابا is an archaic/poetic element here functioning to link or mean 'with' or 'accompanied by' rather than its usual sense (see note), and the coordination with و links the two images.
segments.s527-g41: وزان is a poetic fused prepositional phrase 'from that' (وز + آن); جایگه is 'place/spot' and is the thing being referenced. شد here is the past narrative verb 'became/came' used idiomatically to mean 'arrived/went' (common in classical Persian narrative). به + پرده‌سرای = 'into/to the curtain‑hall' (پرده‌سرا = audience‑chamber/scene); the preposition به indicates movement toward/into the hall.
segments.s527-g42: عرض پیش او رفت is an idiomatic classical construction: عرض (here the noun 'petition/presentation') + پیش 'before/in the presence of' + رفت 'went' = 'a petition was presented before him' (or 'someone presented a petition to him'). با کدخدای marks accompaniment/agent ('with the village‑headman'). Word order places the thing presented (عرض) before the verbal phrase, typical of older Persian.
segments.s527-g43: سپه را uses را as the definite/direct‑object marker (the army = recipient). درم داد is a simple past action 'gave dirhams/money.' و آباد کرد links with a second verb: آباد (prosperous/inhabited) plus کرد ('made') forms the causative 'made prosperous.' The sequence is two coordinated past actions.
segments.s527-g44: ز introduces a genitive/relational phrase in poetic register ('of/from'). دادار is the Creator (a common epithet); نیکی دهش functions as an epithet 'bestower/giver of goodness' or 'good‑giving' modifying دادار. یاد کرد is the verbal phrase 'he remembered/commemorated' (یاد as 'remembrance' + کرد). Overall this is a compact, slightly inverted poetic clause: 'he remembered the Creator, giver of goodness.'
segments.s527-g45: چو here means 'when' (introducing a temporal clause). شد is the past narrative 'became'; لشکرش is the subject 'his army' (لشکر + possessive suffix). چون ... چون forms a simile: 'like/as a brave leopard' (چون = 'like', followed by the epithets دلاور 'valiant' and پلنگ 'leopard').
segments.s527-g46: سوی + name + شد + به جنگ is a classical sequence meaning 'turned/marched toward X to war' or 'went to war against X.' سوی indicates direction; بهمن اردوان is a personal name (Bahman Ardavan); شد is the past narrative verb 'went/turned' and به جنگ ('to battle') states the purpose/scene of the action.
segments.s527-g47: چو گشتند is a temporal clause 'when they became/turned/approached' (گشتند here = 'they came/turned' in the narrative past). نزدیک با یکدگر = 'near/close to one another'; با یکدگر is the reciprocal 'with one another.' The whole segment sets the scene 'when they drew near to each other.'
segments.s527-g48: برفتند is the past plural verb 'they went forth/advanced' (an older poetic past). گردان is the noun 'troop/division' and پرخاشخر is a postpositive adjective/epithet meaning 'aggressive/quarrelsome'; adjective after noun is common in epic verse. Together: 'the quarrelsome troop advanced.'
segments.s527-g1: یکی نامور بود is an existential statement with the indefinite کسی/یکی first ('a certain one'), followed by the predicate adjective نامور and the past copula بود. نامش = نام + ـش is a genitival/possessive construction 'his name' with the clitic ـش attached; سباک is the name (here used as a noun) completing the clause. Word order is somewhat compressed and poetic: 'A certain renowned one was; his name (was) Sabak.'
segments.s527-g2: ابا here introduces a list of possessions/attributes and functions like an accompaniment/possession marker ('having/with'); it precedes a coordinated series آلت و لشکر و رای پاک. آلت ('instrument') refers to implements/arms, لشکر is 'army', and رای پاک is a noun + adjective phrase where پاک follows رای as a postpositive adjective meaning 'pure/unsullied counsel'. The series is linked by simple coordination with و.
segments.s527-g3: که introduces a relative clause modifying the preceding noun ('who/that'). در شهر جهرم is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the city of Jahrom'. The sequence بد او پادشا is a poetic order: بد (adjective 'bad') + او (dative/possessive pronoun 'for him/over him') + پادشا (king), i.e. 'he had a bad king' or literally 'a bad king was over him'.
segments.s527-g4: جهاندیده is an epithet 'worldly‑wise' describing the king. با داد is an instrumental/qualifying phrase 'with justice' (با + داد), and فرمانروا names his office/character 'sovereign/ruler'. The adjectives/epithets are given in a string linked by و, describing the king's qualities.
segments.s527-g5: مر here is an archaic/dative/existential particle ('to/for him' or an emphatic particle) used with او را to indicate possession; او را = 'for him' (literally 'to him'), خجسته پسر is the nominal phrase 'blessed/auspicious son(s)', and بود is the past copula 'there was' or 'he had'. The numeral هفت follows to specify number: 'there were for him seven blessed sons.'
segments.s527-g6: چو = 'when'. آگه شد is a light-verb construction meaning 'became aware' or 'learned' (آگه + شد). از پیش + بهمن means 'in the presence of/ before Bahman' (از پیش = before/in the presence of), and برفت is an older past form meaning 'he went (off)' — here 'he went before Bahman' or 'he went to Bahman'.
segments.s527-g7: ز is an older/pre-poetic form of 'from' marking origin (ز جهرم = 'from Jahrom'). بیامد is the past tense 'came', سوی indicates direction 'toward', and اردشیر is the destination/name. Word order places the origin first, then the verb, then the direction/goal.
segments.s527-g8: This segment lists the accompanying equipment and forces. ابا (here) functions to introduce accompaniment/possession ('with'/'having') before the coordinated list لشکر و کوس و با دار و گیر; لشکر = 'army', کوس = 'war‑drum', با = the preposition 'with' reappears before دار and گیر, and گیر at the end is a noun from the root 'to seize' (here 'grappling/seizing gear' or 'seizers'). The coordination and repetition of با/و is a poetic way to enumerate items.

sentences.s527-s33: They likewise set that sign as far as the city Istakhr, where Bahman had to it renown and pride.
sentences.s527-s34: When the king's voice rose through the world, the army joined from every side.
sentences.s527-s35: They showed him abundant treasure; for Bahman had filled that (place) with toil.
sentences.s527-s36: He scattered the abundant dirhams; by force the army of Pars drove them off.
sentences.s527-s5: When his gaze fell upon the spahbed, from that occasion there arose a reaction, just as was fitting.
sentences.s527-s6: He came forward, kissed the hem of his foot, and remembered the Sasanians still more.
sentences.s527-s7: The world-seeker struck him repeatedly; he recognized his worth for having come promptly.
sentences.s527-s8: Namjuy became anxious because of Sabak; his heart turned full of fear and dread of that old man.
sentences.s527-s25: The army drew up its ranks from both sides; everyone with spear and Indian sword in hand.
sentences.s527-s26: When the warlike lions leapt up, they poured blood like a flowing stream.
sentences.s527-s27: Thus until the sun turned yellow, the air was full of dust and the ground full of men.
sentences.s527-s28: When the heaven's tent turned turquoise, the nimble army came into battle.
sentences.s527-s17: You kept him for me in my father's place, and moreover you were the chief among his notables.
sentences.s527-s18: The king's heart was freed from thought (worry); toward Azar he became gentle and wise.
sentences.s527-s19: He prayed much before God, that there might be for him a good guide.
sentences.s527-s20: In every undertaking he grants him victory; he (also) bestows upon him a great tree.
sentences.s527-s9: There was also an alarm in the inner passage, for with him was a world‑conquering army.
sentences.s527-s10: The old man was worldly‑wise and alert‑hearted; he perceived Ardashir’s intention.
sentences.s527-s11: He came, brought (it), halted and struck; thus he spoke: 'From the exalted Creator...'
sentences.s527-s12: Lowly though he is, well‑built and nimble of soul — unless his heart turns to the pure king.
sentences.s527-s13: When news came from King Ardashir that he had brought an army to this pond.
sentences.s527-s14: I became so thoroughly fed up with Ardavan that even an old woman turned into a young man.
sentences.s527-s15: Consider me well-born and kind, (as) a fetter-holder and a fragrant breeze, and regard me as a knower of secrets.
sentences.s527-s16: When Ardashir heard this speech from him, he conceived another thought.
sentences.s527-s29: A wind rose, and a dust-cloud like tar came forth from the heart of Ardashir's army.
sentences.s527-s30: He flung many maces among them, for they had strength and heart, and also glory and loftiness.
sentences.s527-s31: Bahman Ardavan fled; his body, weary from arrows, ill‑fated.
sentences.s527-s32: Then King Ardashir charged on; with the wailing of trumpets and a shower of arrows.
sentences.s527-s21: From that place he went into the audience-chamber; a petition was brought before him with the village‑headman.
sentences.s527-s22: He gave money to the army and made (the place) prosperous; he remembered the Creator's gracious gift(s).
sentences.s527-s23: When his army became like a brave leopard, he went to war against Bahman Ardavan.
sentences.s527-s24: When they drew near to one another, the quarrelsome troop advanced.
sentences.s527-s1: There was a famed man; his name was Sabak, possessing weapons, an army, and pure counsel.
sentences.s527-s2: Who, in the city of Jahrom, had a bad king — a worldly‑wise sovereign endowed with justice.
sentences.s527-s3: He had seven blessed sons; when he became aware, he went before Bahman.
sentences.s527-s4: From Jahrom he came toward Ardashir, with an army and drums and with standards and grappling gear.
